content.text: Agent runs can fail silently: a worker crashes mid-tool-call, a Claude API request times out, or a tool throws an unhandled exception. Without a
, that job disappears and the user never gets a response. A DLQ captures messages that exceed their retry limit so you can inspect, diagnose, and replay them rather than lose the work permanently.

attrs.alt: DLQ flow: main queue → agent worker → success (delete) or crash×3 → DLQ → inspector

content.text: A standard job queue retries a failed message a fixed number of times and then drops it. For agent runs this is catastrophic: a user submitted a complex research task, the worker crashed once due to a transient API error, and now the task is silently gone. A DLQ instead moves the message to a separate queue after N failures, preserving the full job payload for inspection.
(rate limits, network blips) → retried automatically before the DLQ receives the message
(malformed input, unsupported tool) → exhaust retries and land in DLQ immediately
(worker OOM kill, deployment restart mid-run) → message becomes visible again after visibility_timeout, retried, eventually DLQ'd if persistent
SQS: Setting Up Main Queue and DLQ
AWS SQS is a common choice. The DLQ is just another SQS queue — the main queue's
(the number of delivery attempts before redrive).

Set VisibilityTimeout longer than your longest expected agent run. If a run takes 4 minutes and VisibilityTimeout is 3 minutes, SQS re-delivers the message while the worker is still running — both workers finish and you get a duplicate result.
Embed enough context in the job payload to replay it without querying a database. Include the original request, retry metadata, and a correlation ID for distributed tracing.

from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from datetime import datetime, timezone
import uuid

@dataclass
class AgentJob:
    job_id:      str
    run_id:      str
    tenant_id:   str
    user_id:     str
    messages:    list[dict]   # full Claude messages array
    tools:       list[dict]   # tool definitions
    model:       str
    max_tokens:  int
    created_at:  str
    attempt:     int = 1      # incremented on each retry
    origin:      str = "api"  # "api", "replay", "manual"

    @classmethod
    def new(cls, tenant_id: str, user_id: str,
            messages: list, tools: list) -> "AgentJob":
        return cls(
            job_id=str(uuid.uuid4()),
            run_id=str(uuid.uuid4()),
            tenant_id=tenant_id,
            user_id=user_id,
            messages=messages,
            tools=tools,
            model="claude-opus-4-8",
            max_tokens=4096,
            created_at=datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        )


def enqueue(job: AgentJob) -> str:
    resp = sqs.send_message(
        QueueUrl=main_url,
        MessageBody=json.dumps(asdict(job)),
        MessageGroupId=job.tenant_id,    # FIFO: group per tenant
        MessageDeduplicationId=job.job_id,
    )
    return resp["MessageId"]
model: a received message is hidden from other workers until the receiver deletes it (success) or the timeout expires (re-delivery). Never delete a message until the agent run has fully completed.

import anthropic, time

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def process_job(job: AgentJob, receipt_handle: str) -> None:
    """Run the agent and delete the SQS message on success."""
    try:
        result = run_agent(job)
        # Persist result before deleting the message
        store_result(job.run_id, result)
        # Only delete after successful persistence
        sqs.delete_message(QueueUrl=main_url, ReceiptHandle=receipt_handle)
    except anthropic.RateLimitError:
        # Let the message become visible again; SQS will retry
        # Optionally extend visibility to avoid immediate re-delivery
        sqs.change_message_visibility(
            QueueUrl=main_url,
            ReceiptHandle=receipt_handle,
            VisibilityTimeout=60,  # wait 60s before retry
        )
        raise
    except Exception:
        # Do NOT delete — let SQS count this as a failed delivery
        raise


def worker_loop():
    while True:
        resp = sqs.receive_message(
            QueueUrl=main_url,
            MaxNumberOfMessages=1,
            WaitTimeSeconds=20,       # long polling
            AttributeNames=["ApproximateReceiveCount"],
        )
        messages = resp.get("Messages", [])
        if not messages:
            continue
        msg = messages[0]
        job = AgentJob(**json.loads(msg["Body"]))
        receive_count = int(msg["Attributes"]["ApproximateReceiveCount"])
        job.attempt = receive_count
        process_job(job, msg["ReceiptHandle"])
The DLQ is not a graveyard — it is a staging area for human review. Build a CLI or admin endpoint that reads from the DLQ, classifies the failure, and either replays or discards each message.
Replay enqueues the job back onto the main queue with
so you can filter replays in metrics. Delete the DLQ message
successfully enqueuing the replay — if re-enqueue fails, leave the message in the DLQ.

def replay_job(msg: dict, fix_fn=None) -> str:
    """
    Re-enqueue a DLQ message onto the main queue.
    Optionally apply fix_fn(job) to patch the payload before replay.
    """
    job = AgentJob(**json.loads(msg["Body"]))
    job.origin  = "replay"
    job.attempt = 1        # reset attempt counter
    job.run_id  = str(uuid.uuid4())  # new run ID for fresh tracing

    if fix_fn:
        job = fix_fn(job)

    # Enqueue first, delete from DLQ second
    new_message_id = enqueue(job)
    sqs.delete_message(QueueUrl=dlq_url, ReceiptHandle=msg["ReceiptHandle"])
    return new_message_id


def discard_job(msg: dict, reason: str) -> None:
    """Permanently discard a poison message after logging."""
    job = AgentJob(**json.loads(msg["Body"]))
    log_discarded_job(job, reason)
    sqs.delete_message(QueueUrl=dlq_url, ReceiptHandle=msg["ReceiptHandle"])
on the DLQ. A non-zero count means jobs are failing. Alert immediately — messages in the DLQ are user requests that have not received a response.

import boto3

cloudwatch = boto3.client("cloudwatch", region_name="us-east-1")

def get_dlq_depth() -> int:
    resp = sqs.get_queue_attributes(
        QueueUrl=dlq_url,
        AttributeNames=["ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible"]
    )
    return int(resp["Attributes"]["ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible"])


def check_and_alert():
    depth = get_dlq_depth()
    if depth > 0:
        cloudwatch.put_metric_alarm(
            AlarmName="AgentJobsDLQDepth",
            MetricName="ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible",
            Namespace="AWS/SQS",
            Dimensions=[{"Name": "QueueName", "Value": "agent_jobs_dlq"}],
            Statistic="Sum",
            Period=60,
            EvaluationPeriods=1,
            Threshold=1,
            ComparisonOperator="GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold",
            AlarmActions=["arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123:ops-alerts"],
        )
        print(f"ALERT: DLQ depth = {depth}")
Must exceed the p99 agent run duration
Enough to survive transient errors; not so many that poison messages linger
Gives time for manual inspection before messages expire
Standard; reduce to 1 day for high-volume queues
Long polling — reduces empty-response API calls by 95%
Agent runs are long; batch size 1 avoids starvation

A dead letter queue is the safety net for your agent job pipeline. Configure the main queue's redrive policy to move messages to the DLQ after three delivery attempts. Set VisibilityTimeout longer than your worst-case agent run time to avoid phantom retries. The DLQ is a diagnostic queue, not a discard bin — build an inspector that classifies failures, replays fixable jobs, and discards poison messages after logging. Alert on any non-zero DLQ depth since every message there represents a user request that has not received a response.
